segments.title: Why known destroyer wrecks still need investigation
segments.summary: A question about HMS Mohawk and HMS Bedouin distinguishes a wreck's known location from a site that has received serious archaeological study.
segments.body: Mohawk lies in shallow water, but Dr. Clarke argues that its accessibility has not produced as much investigation as the wreck deserves. Bedouin has received even less attention, illustrating how a ship can be geographically located yet remain historically under-documented.
segments.question: Are HMS Mohawk and HMS Bedouin still lost, or have their wrecks been properly studied?
segments.answerShort: Mohawk's location is known but under-studied, while Bedouin has received almost no serious wreck investigation.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts the known Mohawk site with the limited study of Mohawk and Bedouin.

segments.title: Why finding a warship wreck is only the beginning
segments.summary: A discussion of missing and under-studied destroyer wrecks separates discovery from the historical work needed after a site is located.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes wrecks that remain missing from sites that have been photographed but never properly documented. The useful historical payoff comes from careful surveying, identification, and publication rather than treating the first images as the end of the investigation.

evidence.note: The discussion contrasts locating a wreck with documenting and interpreting it.

segments.title: Could divers study the wreck of HMS Glowworm?
segments.summary: A depth question leads to a proposal for studying deep warship wrecks while exercising specialist naval diving skills.
segments.body: Glowworm is probably too deep for routine recreational scuba and would require remotely operated vehicles or specialized equipment. Dr. Clarke suggests that naval diving teams could combine historical survey work with training for deep-water tasks such as mine clearance and submarine rescue.
segments.question: Is HMS Glowworm's wreck too deep for an ordinary scuba survey?
segments.answerShort: Probably yes; it likely requires specialist diving equipment or an ROV rather than a standard recreational dive.

evidence.note: The response discusses the likely depth, specialist equipment, and a training role for naval divers.

segments.title: Why discovering a wreck also creates a protection problem
segments.summary: The loss of Java Sea wrecks prompts a discussion of the tension between locating historic sites and exposing them to salvage.
segments.body: An undiscovered wreck is harder to loot but also impossible to monitor or protect deliberately. Once a site is identified, maritime authorities and historians gain the information needed to guard it, although enforcement varies greatly between heavily patrolled and lightly monitored seas.
segments.question: Are some warship wrecks safer if they are never found?
segments.answerShort: Remaining hidden can deter looting, but a wreck cannot receive targeted protection until authorities know where it is.

evidence.note: The answer frames discovery and protection as a catch-22 and compares enforcement environments.

segments.title: Basil Jones and the wartime destroyer command track
segments.summary: Basil Jones provides a case study in an inventive destroyer officer whose strengths appeared in operational command rather than staff appointments.
segments.body: The video traces Jones through several destroyer commands and explains why his dislike of paperwork limited his peacetime advancement. His career illustrates how officers could excel at sea without fitting the staff path increasingly required for promotion after the war.

evidence.note: Jones's commands, operational strengths, and avoidance of staff roles are described.

segments.title: Radar, line-abreast tactics, and the Battle of Ushant
segments.summary: Jones experiments with destroyer formations until a difficult line-abreast approach gives radar and forward guns the clearest night-fighting arcs.
segments.body: The watch point follows the 10th Destroyer Flotilla's movement from conventional line-ahead and staggered formations to a practiced line abreast. Minimal-power radar, concentrated Tribal-class gunfire, star shell, and a narrow torpedo profile turned an awkward station-keeping problem into an ambush system.

evidence.note: The formation experiments and tactical benefits of line abreast are explained.

segments.title: Why Basil Jones had room to experiment before D-Day
segments.summary: A viewer questions the freedom given to a Tribal-class flotilla leader during preparations for operations in the Channel.
segments.body: The latitude was not simply a lack of supervision: Philip Vian supported it while holding wider responsibility for one of the D-Day naval sectors. Once at sea, the flotilla could also fall under his operational command, linking Jones's experimentation to an experienced destroyer commander higher in the chain.
segments.question: Why was a Tribal-class flotilla leader given so much operational latitude?
segments.answerShort: Philip Vian supported the arrangement and retained wider operational responsibility, giving experimentation experienced senior cover.

evidence.note: The response explains Vian's advice and his place in the D-Day command structure.

segments.title: Robert Sherbrooke finds his calling in destroyers
segments.summary: Sherbrooke's unusually late move from capital ships and cruisers into destroyers leads to repeated Tribal-class commands and flotilla leadership.
segments.body: After nearly two decades without destroyer service, Sherbrooke impressed Andrew Cunningham in HMS Hereward and moved through Wakeful, Cossack, Afridi, and Matabele. The sequence shows how operational performance could reveal a command specialization that an officer's earlier postings had not predicted.

evidence.note: Sherbrooke's early service and transition into destroyer command are listed.

segments.title: Sherbrooke shields convoy JW 51B in the Barents Sea
segments.summary: Sherbrooke's destroyers repeatedly threaten a much stronger German force, preserving convoy JW 51B despite severe wounds and heavy odds.
segments.body: The account pairs Sherbrooke's Victoria Cross citation with the tactical problem of keeping Admiral Hipper and Lutzow away from fourteen merchant ships. Smoke, torpedo threats, aggressive pursuit, and an orderly transfer of command bought time for the distant cruiser covering force. The failure helped trigger a German crisis of confidence so severe that Hitler effectively kept the heavy surface ships from returning to sea for nearly a year.

evidence.note: The citation, convoy composition, engagement, and tactical comparison are covered.

segments.title: Why JW 51B was defended differently from PQ 17
segments.summary: A viewer asks how the defence of JW 51B contrasts with the earlier PQ 17 disaster.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke avoids blaming the earlier officers for decisions made with different information and orders. By late 1942 the Royal Navy trusted its covering force and had promoted a cohort of exceptionally aggressive destroyer commanders who treated withdrawal as a last resort.
segments.question: Why did the Royal Navy defend JW 51B so differently from convoy PQ 17?
segments.answerShort: Experience after PQ 17, confidence in the covering force, firmer orders, and aggressive flotilla commanders produced a very different response.

evidence.note: The audience comparison prompts an explanation of changing orders, confidence, and command culture.

segments.title: A balanced surface fleet for Poland
segments.summary: A modern force-structure question separates ships optimized for Baltic defence from vessels able to deploy with allies.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes a mixed fleet rather than asking one design to perform incompatible missions. Corvette-sized ships derived from the Orkan or Visby idea would serve local Baltic tasks, while two or three larger frigates would provide endurance for expeditionary operations.
segments.question: What mix of surface combatants would make sense for the Polish Navy?
segments.answerShort: Combine smaller Baltic-focused corvettes with two or three larger frigates for expeditionary work.

evidence.note: The answer recommends distinct local and expeditionary elements for Poland.

segments.title: How weather shaped operations near North Cape
segments.summary: A weather question highlights two very different hazards facing ships in Arctic waters.
segments.body: Heavy seas could exhaust and sicken crews while making weapons, sensors, and formation-keeping harder to manage. Flat calm created a different danger by increasing visibility and making a force easier to detect.
segments.question: How important was weather around North Cape?
segments.answerShort: It was central: rough seas punished crews and ships, while unusual calm made concealment much harder.

evidence.note: The response contrasts violent seas with dangerously revealing calm.

segments.title: William Agnew, Force K, and small-force command
segments.summary: William Agnew's commands link cruiser leadership, Malta's Force K, and eventual command of HMS Vanguard.
segments.body: The career sketch moves from small cruisers to Force K and then to the battleship Vanguard. Dr. Clarke presents the final command as recognition for an officer who repeatedly proved effective with fast, independent naval forces.

evidence.note: Agnew's commands and the significance of Vanguard are introduced.

segments.title: Force K dismantles the Duisburg convoy
segments.summary: Agnew pairs cruisers with destroyers, uses radar and deception, and prioritizes deliberate accuracy in a devastating night attack on an Axis convoy.
segments.body: Force K approached under moonlight as cruiser-destroyer teams, reduced speed, and opened fire at close range after using a conspicuously powerful signal to misdirect Italian searches. Jamming, radar, and accurate salvos helped sink roughly 40,000 tons of shipping while the British force escaped with little damage.

evidence.note: The force composition, deception plan, engagement sequence, and result are described.

segments.title: Why sea-going officers leave as command posts shrink
segments.summary: A discussion of promotion argues that shrinking fleets also remove the sea commands that retain action-oriented officers.
segments.body: As officers reach commander, captain, and flag rank, the available jobs become increasingly staff-heavy. Dr. Clarke argues that carrier groups, escort groups, and amphibious task groups need senior seagoing posts not only for command effectiveness but also to retain officers motivated by operations rather than paperwork.

evidence.note: The stream connects officer attrition to the scarcity of senior sea commands.

segments.title: Philip Vian's path from destroyers to the British Pacific Fleet
segments.summary: Vian's commands span destroyer flotillas, cruisers, carriers, amphibious operations, and the Home Fleet.
segments.body: The career overview shows an officer repeatedly selected when the Royal Navy faced a difficult operational problem. His experience expanded from Tribal-class destroyers and the Altmark incident through Torch, D-Day, carrier command, and the British Pacific Fleet.

evidence.note: Vian's commands and major operational assignments are summarized.

segments.title: The selective command course for surface-warfare officers
segments.summary: A comparison with the submarine Perisher course highlights the Royal Navy's less mythologized but similarly demanding surface command training.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes the surface-warfare command course as highly selective in its own right. It attracts less mystique than Perisher largely because a greater number of officers must pass through it before taking surface commands, not because the standard is undemanding.

evidence.note: The surface command course is compared with Perisher in selectivity and reputation.

segments.title: Did Philip Vian lack the aviation experience to command carriers?
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether criticism of Vian's limited aviation background is fair.
segments.body: The answer is qualified: Vian drove aircrews hard, but Mediterranean conditions and the need for continuous support constrained his choices. Fleet Air Arm testimony also credited him with accepting risk to recover aviators, while crowded British carriers made any embarked admiral's staff contentious.
segments.question: Was Philip Vian's lack of aviation experience a serious weakness as a carrier-force commander?
segments.answerShort: It created friction, but operational constraints explain much of his severity and many aviators respected his commitment to recovering them.

evidence.note: The question prompts a balanced assessment of Vian's carrier leadership.

segments.title: Vian boards Altmark inside a Norwegian fjord
segments.summary: HMS Cossack forces its way into Jossingfjord, boards Altmark, and frees prisoners taken by Admiral Graf Spee.
segments.body: The episode reconstructs the British search, the legal problem created by prisoners in a neutral port, and Cossack's close-quarters boarding under searchlights and attempted ramming. The operation recovered the prisoners but deliberately stopped short of taking the German ship as a prize.

evidence.note: The search, fjord entry, boarding, and prisoner recovery are narrated.

segments.title: Why did Vian free Altmark's prisoners but leave the ship?
segments.summary: A viewer asks why Cossack did not capture Altmark after the boarding succeeded.
segments.body: Taking the vessel from Norwegian internal waters would have deepened an already serious violation of neutrality. Recovering British prisoners offered enough moral and political justification for the operation without turning it into outright seizure of the ship.
segments.question: Why did Vian not capture Altmark as a prize?
segments.answerShort: Freeing the prisoners was politically defensible; stealing the damaged ship from Norwegian waters would have been much harder to justify.

evidence.note: The answer distinguishes prisoner recovery from seizing the vessel.

segments.title: What weapons did Altmark's boarding parties carry?
segments.summary: A question about cutlasses opens a discussion of the mixed weapons and training used by cruiser and destroyer boarding parties.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke suggests cruiser personnel were more likely to carry swords, while destroyer sailors favored pistols, rifles, knives, and improvised close-combat tools. The difference reflected both tradition and the broad technical duties expected from the smaller officer complement in a destroyer.
segments.question: Did the boarding crew at the Altmark incident carry cutlasses?
segments.answerShort: Some cruiser personnel may have carried swords, but destroyer parties more often relied on firearms, knives, and improvised close-combat weapons.

evidence.note: The stream compares likely cruiser and destroyer boarding weapons.

segments.title: How the Royal Navy found Altmark
segments.summary: A question about a conveniently overheard Norwegian conversation tests one story about how Altmark's location reached Britain.
segments.body: Sympathetic Norwegian officers may have tried to leak the ship's position near a British diplomat, but that report arrived too late to guide the interception. Dr. Clarke says the British had already located Altmark, probably aided by access to Norwegian communications.
segments.question: Did Norwegian officers reveal Altmark's position by speaking near a British diplomat?
segments.answerShort: They may have tried, but the message arrived after the British had already found Altmark through other intelligence.

evidence.note: The answer separates the late diplomatic leak from the intelligence that had already located the ship.

segments.title: How the British leadership reacted to Vian's Altmark raid
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether Vian's breach of Norwegian neutrality damaged his standing with the British government.
segments.body: The political risk did not derail Vian's career because the operation recovered British prisoners and was judged to have been handled decisively. Churchill, Dudley Pound, and Andrew Cunningham thereafter treated him as a leading choice when the Royal Navy faced a difficult operational problem.
segments.question: What was the British government's reaction to Vian after the Altmark incident?
segments.answerShort: Churchill strongly approved, and Vian became an early choice for later difficult assignments.

evidence.note: The response describes Churchill's approval and Vian's later standing with senior leaders.

segments.title: How the Altmark incident accelerated Germany's Norway plans
segments.summary: A viewer asks how the Altmark incident affected later German operations.
segments.body: The incident accelerated planning for the invasion of Norway and denied Germany a major propaganda success. Recovering roughly 300 merchant sailors also returned scarce manpower to Britain's Battle of the Atlantic effort.
segments.question: How did the Altmark incident affect later German operations?
segments.answerShort: It hastened the timetable for invading Norway while denying Germany prisoners and a valuable psychological victory.

evidence.note: The answer links Altmark to Weserubung planning and British manpower.

segments.title: Why Royal Navy crews still trained for boarding and fighting ashore
segments.summary: A question about boarding practice connects the Altmark action to the Royal Navy's interwar experience with landing parties and ship defence.
segments.body: Royal Navy crews were not improvised infantry, but they received substantial small-arms and boarding instruction. Interwar deployments had also required repeated operations ashore, while even modern crews retained training for defending their own ship against a boarding attempt.
segments.question: Were Royal Navy crews deliberately trained for boarding actions?
segments.answerShort: Yes; boarding, rifle drill, landing parties, and defence of the ship remained part of naval training.

evidence.note: The answer links boarding proficiency to interwar land actions, rifle drill, and ship defence.

segments.title: Why sailors, not Royal Marines, dominated the Altmark boarding
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether the boarding party would naturally have been composed mainly of Royal Marines.
segments.body: Destroyers did not normally carry the sizeable Royal Marine detachment found in a cruiser. Although personnel came across from the cruiser force, the available boarding parties were still dominated by sailors, whose shipboard duties and training already included this kind of close action.
segments.question: Were Royal Marines the main force in the Altmark boarding party?
segments.answerShort: No; destroyers carried few Marines, and even the cruiser-supplied parties included many sailors.

evidence.note: The response explains destroyer complements and the sailor-heavy composition of the boarding parties.

segments.title: Gerard Roope and HMS Glowworm's last fight
segments.summary: Glowworm fights two destroyers, attacks Admiral Hipper with torpedoes, and finally rams the German cruiser before sinking.
segments.body: Roope correctly recognized that the destroyers were drawing him toward a heavier force but chose to report and attack because heavy weather made shadowing impractical. Glowworm's torpedoes narrowly missed Admiral Hipper before the destroyer rammed and continued firing at point-blank range. The Victoria Cross case depended heavily on the German commander's postwar account of the action.

evidence.note: Roope's citation and the action against Admiral Hipper are recounted.

segments.title: What happened to the destroyers that drew Glowworm toward Hipper?
segments.summary: A brief follow-up traces the two German destroyers from their encounter with Glowworm into the fighting at Narvik.
segments.body: The destroyers escaped Glowworm and continued with the invasion force rather than being destroyed in the encounter. Their reprieve was short-lived because both were then caught up in the battles around Narvik.
segments.question: What happened to the German destroyers after they drew Glowworm toward Admiral Hipper?
segments.answerShort: They escaped the immediate action but soon became involved in the battles of Narvik.

evidence.note: The answer follows the destroyers from the Glowworm action to Narvik.

segments.title: Why Glowworm's sacrifice still had tactical value
segments.summary: The loss of a destroyer is weighed against the chance to damage or expose a German heavy cruiser that the Kriegsmarine could not readily replace.
segments.body: Britain could absorb the loss of a G-class destroyer more easily than Germany could lose Admiral Hipper, and even damage could remove the cruiser for months. The discussion also traces how one altered encounter might have changed German movements off Norway and the later fate of HMS Glorious.

evidence.note: The tactical exchange rate and possible wider consequences are assessed.

segments.title: Did heavy weather give Glowworm an advantage?
segments.summary: A question about seakeeping compares Glowworm with heavily armed German destroyers in the Norwegian Sea.
segments.body: Glowworm was not an exceptional heavy-weather ship, but she handled the conditions better than the German destroyers. Their large weapons and accumulated topweight made them particularly uncomfortable and less effective in rough seas, while Tribal-class ships represented the stronger British answer for sustained high-sea work.
segments.question: How much did Glowworm's seakeeping help against the German destroyers?
segments.answerShort: She was not ideal in those seas, but she handled them better than the top-heavy German destroyers.

evidence.note: The first answer compares Glowworm's handling with Tribal-class destroyers.
The follow-up attributes German difficulty to heavy armament and topweight.

segments.title: Frederick Peters returns to war for special service
segments.summary: A retired First World War commander returns in 1939 and volunteers for a hazardous special-service mission at age fifty-three.
segments.body: Peters had retired in 1919 after winning the Distinguished Service Cross, then came back to command an anti-submarine vessel and later special operations. His age and voluntary return make the physical leadership demanded at Oran especially striking.

evidence.note: Peters's earlier service, retirement, return, and age are described.

segments.title: Peters drives HMS Walney into Oran harbor
segments.summary: Operation Reservist sends two former Coast Guard cutters through Oran's boom to land troops directly against overwhelming defenses.
segments.body: The planners sent two lightly armed former United States Coast Guard cutters and 393 troops into a harbor defended by thousands of soldiers, shore batteries, and 31 warships because seizing the port intact offered an important operational prize. Peters remained on Walney's exposed bridge, brought the burning ship alongside, and personally helped secure lines so troops could disembark. The mission suffered catastrophic casualties and failed to preserve the harbor facilities, yet his conduct under point-blank fire earned the Victoria Cross and an American Distinguished Service Cross.

evidence.note: The citations, force imbalance, harbor defenses, casualties, and result are presented.

segments.title: How earlier carrier losses left Force Z without air cover
segments.summary: A question about Royal Navy overstretch leads to a counterfactual linking Courageous, Glorious, and Ark Royal to Force Z's missing carrier.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the survival of even one earlier carrier could have freed another hull for the Far East, while two survivors would make carrier support much more likely. He treats the three losses as failures in escort, employment, or damage control rather than proof that British carrier doctrine was generally backward.
segments.question: How did Royal Navy overstretch and earlier carrier losses affect Force Z?
segments.answerShort: Losing Courageous, Glorious, and Ark Royal removed the reserve capacity that might have placed a carrier with Force Z.

evidence.note: The answer connects earlier carrier losses to the lack of Force Z air cover.

segments.title: Why HMS Indomitable did not reach Force Z
segments.summary: A viewer asks why HMS Indomitable was absent when Force Z deployed.
segments.body: Indomitable's grounding was only part of the delay: she also had to work up, embark a new air group, and remain available for possible Mediterranean demands. British planners expected Force Z to deter Japan and did not anticipate an immediate attack on the scale that followed.
segments.question: Why was HMS Indomitable not operating with Force Z?
segments.answerShort: Grounding, work-up and air-group delays, competing Mediterranean needs, and mistaken deterrence assumptions kept her away.

evidence.note: The response lists Indomitable's delay and the strategic assumptions behind Force Z.

segments.title: Were British aircraft carriers treated as capital units?
segments.summary: A question about carrier status opens a compact comparison of doctrine and the failures behind three wartime carrier losses.
segments.body: The Royal Navy did treat carriers as capital units and expected Swordfish patrols to provide warning while a strike group remained ready. Glorious lacked that posture, Courageous was risked in an anti-submarine role, and Ark Royal's loss exposed the consequences of poor counter-flooding and damage control in a top-heavy ship.
segments.question: Did the Royal Navy regard aircraft carriers as capital units?
segments.answerShort: Yes; doctrine emphasized airborne warning and ready strike aircraft, though employment and damage-control failures sometimes defeated it.

evidence.note: The answer describes carrier doctrine and contrasts three losses.

segments.title: Why the Falklands restored the case for naval guns
segments.summary: A short doctrinal aside warns against assuming that missile warfare will keep ships permanently beyond gun range.
segments.body: British planners had treated guns as increasingly unnecessary because future naval combat was expected to occur at missile range. The Falklands War demonstrated that ships still had to operate close enough for naval gunfire and other short-range tasks, exposing the danger of designing a fleet around only one predicted form of combat.

evidence.note: The discussion uses the Falklands War to challenge the assumption that missiles make naval guns unnecessary.

segments.title: Peters volunteers when asked to nominate ships for danger
segments.summary: A reported origin story for Operation Reservist has Peters nominate himself after judging the assignment likely to be suicidal.
segments.body: Asked to provide two ships for special operations, Peters reportedly chose his own command and sought a second volunteer rather than assigning the danger to subordinates. The video then follows his death in a Sunderland crash after surviving Oran and notes the memorial that bears his name in British Columbia.

evidence.note: The nomination story and Peters's later death are recounted with an explicit source caveat.

segments.title: Were Tribal-class destroyers refitted in American yards?
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether Tribal-class destroyers used East Coast American shipyards for refits.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke does not recall any Tribal refits in the United States and instead points to Britain, India, and South Africa. American yards were more often reserved for larger ships whose repair justified the longer movement.
segments.question: Did any Tribal-class destroyers receive refits in East Coast American yards?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; their known refits were mainly in Britain, India, and South Africa, while larger ships were sent to New York.

evidence.note: The answer compares likely Tribal refit locations with ships sent to New York.

segments.title: How much better was Renown than Repulse by the Second World War?
segments.summary: A brief answer identifies reconstruction as the decisive difference between the two battlecruisers.
segments.body: Renown had received the more comprehensive modernization and was therefore the markedly stronger ship by the Second World War. The comparison points readers toward refit history rather than treating sister ships as permanently equivalent.
segments.question: How much better was HMS Renown than HMS Repulse during the Second World War?
segments.answerShort: Renown was much better because she had received the major modernization that Repulse lacked.

evidence.note: The answer attributes Renown's advantage to her greater modernization.

segments.title: The American disguise used at Oran
segments.summary: The attacking force tried to present the harbor penetration as an American operation rather than another British attack on the French fleet.
segments.body: The choice of former American cutters, embarked United States troops, and American flags was deliberate. British planners hoped that emphasizing an American identity might reduce French resistance after the bitter memory of the Royal Navy's 1940 attack at Mers-el-Kebir.

evidence.note: The answer identifies the ships, troops, and flags used to make the operation appear American.

segments.title: What naval historians learn from commanders' families
segments.summary: A viewer asks whether meeting the families of the featured officers adds value to naval-history research.
segments.body: Family conversations can reveal details absent from archives and published scholarship, but Dr. Clarke distinguishes those recollections from claims supported by multiple academic or archival sources. The answer is also a useful guide to how he signals different levels of evidentiary confidence in a live lecture.
segments.question: Would meeting the families of the commanders discussed improve the research?
segments.answerShort: Yes; relatives can supply unique context, provided their recollections are clearly distinguished from archival and multi-source evidence.

evidence.note: The response explains the value and evidentiary limits of family testimony.

segments.title: How a nineteenth-century gunboat was winterized
segments.summary: A question about a gunboat in Toronto Harbor covers both conversion time and the practical meaning of winterization before modern lubricants.
segments.body: A ship already assigned to the Canada station might need two or three weeks, while a newly arrived vessel could require roughly six weeks. For an age-of-sail-era ship, the work centered on keeping the crew and working spaces warm by covering gunports, rigging tarpaulins over openings, and extending normal North Atlantic preparations.
segments.question: How long would winterizing an 1860s gunboat take, and what did the conversion involve?
segments.answerShort: About two to three weeks for a locally based ship or up to six weeks for a new arrival, chiefly to enclose openings and keep crew spaces workable in severe cold.

evidence.note: The answer gives estimated conversion times and describes cold-weather coverings.
